summary: Gruber answers reader questions about the iPhone 11, iPhone 11 Pro, and Apple Watch Series 5 in a practical Q&A format. He covers iPhone topics including the Quick Start transfer feature, Face ID with sunglasses, Night Mode vs. Deep Fusion, camera lens choices, and the new QuickTake video feature. On the Apple Watch side, he addresses the always-on display's hardware requirements, Theater Mode behavior, sleep behavior, and notification privacy. The post serves as a companion piece to his formal reviews, addressing the specific practical concerns buyers have during launch weekend.
key_points: Quick Start iPhone-to-iPhone transfer works from iOS 12.4.1 and doesn't require updating the old phone to iOS 13
Night Mode handles truly dark environments (under 10 lux) while Deep Fusion targets mid-level lighting (600-10 lux), with Deep Fusion expected in iOS 13.2
The always-on display is exclusive to Series 5 due to a new hardware display driver supporting variable refresh rates down to 1Hz — zero chance of coming to older watches
The ultra-wide 0.5× lens on the regular iPhone 11 is a better choice than a telephoto for typical users, though it depends on shooting style
QuickTake changed volume button behavior so holding shoots video instead of burst photos, with no setting to revert — which Gruber thinks should be offered
money_quotes: No, there is no setting for this. There should be, though.
This feature can seem like magic with certain slightly crooked photos.
Zero chance. The power efficiency comes from a new (hardware) display driver that supports variable refresh rates down to 1Hz (one update per second). Only Series 5 watches have this hardware.
Notifications do not appear on screen in low-power mode.
In a dark room, the always-on display remains just bright enough to be legible, but it's so dim that it doesn't glow.
key_insight: Gruber's rapid-fire Q&A format distills the practical launch-day concerns about iPhone 11 and Apple Watch Series 5 into definitive, experience-backed answers that his formal reviews didn't cover.
